pages.full_text:
__Saturday, 14th August__
	We drove into __Carcasson__, a fortified town, which was once taken by Simon de Montfort. The walls were originally Gallo-Roman, the usual square stones with red bricks, but in about 1300, the builders put new, massive blocks of stone above & below the earlier construction, to strengthen it. The towers are this [oval shape drawing] shape, for strength, & entrances are limited & overlooked by lots of arrow slits.
	The church has fabulous stained glass, trees of Jesse, & Life & 2 magnificent rose windows, lovely statues & a big square carving (sarcophagus) of The Siege of a city — Crusader helmets & an angel. The Seig Stone
	Gorgeous shops & I bought a 3m. tablecloth with blue grapes.
	We had lunch next to the Canal du Midi — quite small, that [struck] links Med. wi. Atlanti[c]
pages.notes: The town name is written 'Carcasson' (single 'n' at end); likely Carcassonne. 'The Seig Stone' appears as a marginal annotation in smaller writing beside the sarcophagus sentence. A small hand-drawn oval appears in-line to illustrate tower shape. 'Simon de Montfort' may also be 'Simon de Montfort'. The bottom line is partially cut off at the right edge; reads '...that [struck-through word] links Med. wi. Atlanti[c]' — the struck-through word looks like 'the' or 'that'. Year cannot be determined from this page alone; volume B covers 2003–2019.

pages.full_text:
[Glued-in aerial postcard photo of Carcassonne with caption on postcard: Carcasson Aude]
↑ this area between the 2 walls was used for trying in fighting & jousting

As we go north, the hills are covered in woods.
The GAS computer map is useful. Simon can choose a carpark in a town & plot it on
pages.notes: The right page contains a large glued-in aerial postcard photograph of Carcassonne. The postcard itself bears the printed text 'Carcasson Aude'. A handwritten annotation with an arrow points to the area between the two walls: 'this area between the 2 walls was used for trying in fighting & jousting' — 'trying in' may be [?training in] or [?trying in]; transcribed verbatim. The text at the bottom of the page is cut off ('plot it on'). 'GAS computer map' likely refers to a sat-nav or GPS device brand. Year cannot be confirmed from page content alone.
